You are an adversarial production-readiness assessor for the hc-assay engine at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay (a dataset-agnostic, data-sovereign empirical-ML/methodology engine; optional backends import-guarded; transcripts committed + scrubbed intentionally). It has survived EIGHT hardening passes and CI is currently green — easy findings are gone. Report ONLY genuine, concrete, FALSIFIABLE production-readiness defects with a real failure mode and a file:line/symbol location; no style nits. If a dimension is clean, return few/zero findings — do NOT invent. Read real code (Read/Grep/Bash; "python -m pytest", "source .venv/bin/activate"). The branch is harden/pass-9 (just branched from merged main @ pass 8).

YOUR DIMENSION: actual CI execution (NEW priority).
NEW DIMENSION born from pass 8: the assessment repeatedly checked CODE but never whether CI actually runs green, so CI was silently red for ~3 passes. Inspect the REAL CI: read .github/workflows/ci.yml and reason about each lane's EXACT command as it runs in CI (bare `pytest` not `python -m pytest`; the no-extras `core` lane with ONLY requirements-core.lock installed; the cold-cache `uv` resolve; the matrix py3.11–3.14). Look for: tests that import optional backends WITHOUT pytest.importorskip and would fail in the no-extras core lane (grep tests for bare `import`/`from` of opentelemetry/httpx/mlflow/langgraph/langchain_ollama/qdrant_client/psycopg/numpy/anyio/phoenix/claude_agent_sdk/openinference NOT preceded by importorskip); any gate that can pass vacuously or fail nondeterministically; any lane whose install step could fail on hashes; coverage/audit/license gate gaps. You may run `gh run list --branch main` / `gh run view` to inspect actual outcomes. A test that only runs in one lane and is unguarded for another is a finding.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
name: ci

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:

# Least privilege (#H-008): the default GITHUB_TOKEN can be repo-write; this workflow only reads
# the code and uploads artifacts, so pin the token to read-only at the top level. Any job needing
# more must opt in explicitly with its own `permissions:`.
permissions:
  contents: read

jobs:
  # Dependency-free core: the engine must lint, type-check, and test with NO extras installed
  # (optional backends are import-guarded; integration tests skip). This guards ADR-0006.
  core:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    strategy:
      matrix:
        python-version: ["3.11", "3.12", "3.13", "3.14"]
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@34e114876b0b11c390a56381ad16ebd13914f8d5 # v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@a26af69be951a213d495a4c3e4e4022e16d87065 # v5
        with:
          python-version: ${{ matrix.python-version }}
      - name: Install (no extras, hash-pinned)
        run: |
          # Install the dev toolchain from the hashed, audited core lockfile so tests run against
          # EXACTLY the versions pip-audit scanned and the SBOM describes — not a live PyPI
          # resolution where a malicious patch release could execute under test (#F-004). The
          # core lock carries only the `dev` extra (no backends), preserving the ADR-0006 guard.
          python -m pip install --upgrade pip
          pip install --require-hashes -r requirements-core.lock
          pip install --require-hashes -r requirements-build.lock  # hashed build backend (#J-003)
          pip install --no-build-isolation --no-deps -e .
      - name: Lint (ruff)
        run: ruff check src tests
      - name: Format check (ruff)
        run: ruff format --check src tests
      - name: Type-check (mypy)
        run: mypy
      - name: Test (pytest)
        run: pytest -q

  # Extras lane: install the real backends and run the integration suite so the backend code
  # paths actually execute, and mypy type-checks against the REAL (not ignored) library types.
  # Service-dependent tests (ollama/qdrant/postgres) skip when the service is absent in CI; the
  # extra-only tests (langgraph graph, mlflow lifecycle, OTel, numpy primitives, methodology
  # e2e) run for real.
  integration:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    services:
      postgres:
        image: postgres:16
        env:
          POSTGRES_PASSWORD: postgres
          POSTGRES_DB: assay
        ports: ["5432:5432"]
        options: >-
          --health-cmd "pg_isready -U postgres"
          --health-interval 5s --health-timeout 5s --health-retries 10
      qdrant:
        image: qdrant/qdrant:v1.12.4
        ports: ["6333:6333"]
    env:
      # loopback host keeps the data-sovereignty guard satisfied; creds are redacted on error
      ASSAY_TEST_POSTGRES_URL: postgresql://[REDACTED]@localhost:5432/assay
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@34e114876b0b11c390a56381ad16ebd13914f8d5 # v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@a26af69be951a213d495a4c3e4e4022e16d87065 # v5
        with:
          python-version: "3.12"
      - name: Install (all extras, hash-pinned)
        run: |
          # Install from the hashed all-extras lockfile so the integration suite executes against
          # exactly the audited, pinned versions — closing the window where a compromised patch
          # release within a pyproject range could run during the real-backend tests (#F-004).
          python -m pip install --upgrade pip
          pip install --require-hashes -r requirements.lock
          pip install --require-hashes -r requirements-build.lock  # hashed build backend (#J-003)
          pip install --no-build-isolation --no-deps -e .
      - name: Type-check against real backend types (mypy)
        run: mypy
      - name: Verify service containers are reachable (no silent skip)
        # #H-009: the live Postgres/Qdrant tests SKIP when the service is unreachable, so a
        # misconfigured/never-started container would let the integration job report GREEN without
        # actually exercising the backends. Fail loudly here if either service isn't up, so the
        # gate cannot pass vacuously.
        run: |
          set -euo pipefail
          for i in $(seq 1 30); do
            pg_isready -h localhost -p 5432 -U postgres && break || sleep 2
            [ "$i" = "30" ] && { echo "::error::Postgres service never became ready"; exit 1; }
          done
          for i in $(seq 1 30); do
            curl -fsS http://localhost:6333/readyz && break || sleep 2
            [ "$i" = "30" ] && { echo "::error::Qdrant service never became ready"; exit 1; }
          done
          echo "postgres + qdrant reachable"
      # Real backends now run: langgraph graph + interrupt/resume, mlflow lifecycle, OTel,
      # numpy primitives, the T3 drain/classification (synthetic SDK, no metered call), AND —
      # via the service containers — the live Postgres checkpointer + Qdrant vector store.
      # Ollama and the metered frontier tier are not in CI and skip honestly.
      - name: Integration + unit tests with coverage
        # --cov-fail-under ENFORCES a floor (#K-OPS-3): without it `pytest --cov` prints coverage
        # but exits 0 at any level, so the coverage "gate" was vacuous — a refactor deleting tests
        # or adding a large untested module would still pass green. The floor (90) sits below the
        # current ~97% with headroom for environment-dependent skips; ratchet it up over time.
        run: pytest -q --cov=assay_engine --cov-report=term-missing --cov-fail-under=90

  # Supply-chain: fail on known-vulnerable dependencies (a security/privacy engine must not ship
  # with advisories). The scan runs against the committed, fully-pinned, hashed lockfile
  # (requirements.lock) so the audit is reproducible across runs and matches what installs
  # (#108, #130, #131, #132, #145, #146). An allowlist file (.pip-audit-ignore, one advisory id
  # per line, '#' comments and blank lines allowed) covers accepted/unfixable ones.
  audit:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@34e114876b0b11c390a56381ad16ebd13914f8d5 # v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@a26af69be951a213d495a4c3e4e4022e16d87065 # v5
        with:
          python-version: "3.12"
      - name: Install pip-audit
        # Pin the audit tooling to exact versions (#G-022): an unpinned pip-audit can change
        # advisory results / exit behavior between releases, making the supply-chain gate
        # non-reproducible (a green commit could fail tomorrow on a tooling bump) — the analogue
        # of the uv pin (#F-031).
        run: python -m pip install --upgrade pip && python -m pip install pip-audit==2.10.1
      - name: Lockfiles are in sync with pyproject (reproducibility guard)
        run: |
          # Pin uv to the exact version the lockfiles were generated with (#F-031): an unpinned
          # `uv` upgrade can change output format/ordering/annotations and produce spurious diffs
          # that fail this gate on every PR even when the lockfiles are current.
          python -m pip install uv==0.11.21
          # Pin the RESOLUTION to a fixed point-in-time index (#K-OPS-1). Without --exclude-newer,
          # uv resolves the newest in-range version of every (largely unconstrained, transitive)
          # dep at CI wall-clock time, so this "reproducibility" gate goes red the instant ANY
          # upstream publishes an in-range release — on PRs that touch no dependency. The date is
          # the single source of truth in .uv-exclude-newer (regenerate_lockfiles.sh reads the same
          # file), so the regen here matches the committed lock exactly. Bump that file to update.
          EXCLUDE_NEWER="$(tr -d '[:space:]' < .uv-exclude-newer)"
          uv pip compile pyproject.toml --all-extras --universal --generate-hashes --exclude-newer "$EXCLUDE_NEWER" -o /tmp/requirements.regen.lock
          # ignore the auto-generated header (timestamp/command line) when diffing
          diff <(grep -vE '^[[:space:]]*#' requirements.lock) <(grep -vE '^[[:space:]]*#' /tmp/requirements.regen.lock) \
            || { echo "::error::requirements.lock is stale — run: scripts/regenerate_lockfiles.sh"; exit 1; }
          # the core lockfile (dev extra only) backs the hash-pinned core lane (#F-004) and must
          # stay in sync too.
          uv pip compile pyproject.toml --extra dev --universal --generate-hashes --exclude-newer "$EXCLUDE_NEWER" -o /tmp/requirements-core.regen.lock
          diff <(grep -vE '^[[:space:]]*#' requirements-core.lock) <(grep -vE '^[[:space:]]*#' /tmp/requirements-core.regen.lock) \
            || { echo "::error::requirements-core.lock is stale — run: scripts/regenerate_lockfiles.sh"; exit 1; }
          # the build-backend lockfile (hatchling etc.) backs the hash-pinned --no-build-isolation
          # editable install (#J-003) and must stay in sync.
          printf 'hatchling\neditables\n' > /tmp/build-reqs.in
          uv pip compile /tmp/build-reqs.in --universal --generate-hashes --exclude-newer "$EXCLUDE_NEWER" -o /tmp/requirements-build.regen.lock
          diff <(grep -vE '^[[:space:]]*#' requirements-build.lock) <(grep -vE '^[[:space:]]*#' /tmp/requirements-build.regen.lock) \
            || { echo "::error::requirements-build.lock is stale — run: scripts/regenerate_lockfiles.sh"; exit 1; }
      - name: pip-audit (reproducible known-vulnerability scan)
        run: |
          set -euo pipefail
          # --strict: any dependency that cannot be collected/audited FAILS the gate (no silent
          # skips). --require-hashes: every pin must carry a hash. Build --ignore-vuln args as an
          # array so allowlist entries can't word-split or glob-expand, and read the file
          # newline-agnostically so the last line is honored even without a trailing newline.
          IGNORES=()
          if [ -f .pip-audit-ignore ]; then
            while IFS= read -r id || [ -n "$id" ]; do
              case "$id" in ''|\#*) continue ;; esac
              IGNORES+=(--ignore-vuln "$id")
            done < .pip-audit-ignore
          fi
          pip-audit --strict --require-hashes --progress-spinner off -r requirements.lock "${IGNORES[@]}"
      - name: SBOM (CycloneDX) from the lockfile
        run: |
          set -euo pipefail
          # Emit a machine-readable software bill of materials for the pinned dependency set so
          # downstream license/transitive monitoring has a stable artifact to track (#147). No
          # `|| true` mask (#F-012): a pip-audit failure must fail the step, and the SBOM must be
          # non-empty, so a broken/absent SBOM cannot pass CI green.
          # Apply the SAME allowlist as the gate (#G-015): pip-audit exits non-zero on ANY finding
          # regardless of output format, so without the allowlist an ACCEPTED advisory would fail
          # SBOM generation here even though the gate step passed — coupling SBOM emission to vuln
          # status and making the allowlist ineffective. The gate step already ran first, so a
          # NON-allowlisted advisory has failed the job before we reach here.
          IGNORES=()
          if [ -f .pip-audit-ignore ]; then
            while IFS= read -r id || [ -n "$id" ]; do
              case "$id" in ''|\#*) continue ;; esac
              IGNORES+=(--ignore-vuln "$id")
            done < .pip-audit-ignore
          fi
          pip-audit --progress-spinner off -r requirements.lock --format cyclonedx-json -o sbom.cyclonedx.json "${IGNORES[@]}"
          test -s sbom.cyclonedx.json
          echo "SBOM emitted ($(wc -c < sbom.cyclonedx.json) bytes)"
      - name: Upload SBOM artifact
        if: always()
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@ea165f8d65b6e75b540449e92b4886f43607fa02 # v4
        with:
          name: sbom-cyclonedx
          path: sbom.cyclonedx.json
          if-no-files-found: error
      - name: License gate (deny strong-copyleft in the pinned dependency set)
        run: |
          set -euo pipefail
          # Fail if any dependency carries a strong-copyleft license incompatible with the
          # Apache-2.0 distribution (#147). Scan the SAME hash-pinned set pip-audit audited, not a
          # live `pip install -e .[all]` resolution that could pull different versions than the
          # lockfile pins (#F-013). Advisory monitoring of the rest is handled by Dependabot.
          pip install --require-hashes -r requirements.lock
          pip install --require-hashes -r requirements-build.lock  # hashed build backend (#J-003)
          pip install --no-build-isolation --no-deps -e .
          python -m pip install pip-licenses==5.5.5  # pinned for reproducible license output (#G-022)
          pip-licenses --format=json > licenses.json  # scan the dep set, not system tooling (#J-006)
          python scripts/license_gate.py licenses.json
      - name: Upload license report
        if: always()
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@ea165f8d65b6e75b540449e92b4886f43607fa02 # v4
        with:
          name: license-report
          path: licenses.json
          if-no-files-found: warn

  # Single required status check (#F-014): branch protection should require ONLY `all-checks`.
  # It fans in every other job, so adding a new job automatically gates merges without anyone
  # remembering to update the branch-protection rule — and `audit`/`integration` can never be
  # silently dropped from the merge gate by requiring just `core`.
  all-checks:
    if: always()
    needs: [core, integration, audit]
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Verify all required jobs succeeded
        run: |
          results="${{ needs.core.result }} ${{ needs.integration.result }} ${{ needs.audit.result }}"
          echo "job results: $results"
          for r in $results; do
            if [ "$r" != "success" ]; then
              echo "::error::a required job did not succeed ($r)"
              exit 1
            fi
          done
          echo "all required jobs succeeded"

content.content:
82:[tool.pytest.ini_options]
83-testpaths = ["tests"]
84-# "src" puts the package on the path; "." (the rootdir) puts the repo root on sys.path so the
85-# `tests` package itself is importable (several suites do `from tests import reference_study` and
86-# `from tests.integration.conftest import ...`). Without it, the bare `pytest` console script CI
87-# runs — unlike `python -m pytest`, which prepends CWD — fails with `ModuleNotFoundError: No
88-# module named 'tests'`, so the whole CI test step errored at collection (#K-CI-2).
89-pythonpath = ["src", "."]
90-filterwarnings = [
91-    # The unkeyed-trail advisory (#F-049) is intentional and fires whenever run_study is called
92-    # without a secret — most tests deliberately use the unkeyed reproducible path. Suppress the
93-    # noise here; test_run_study_warns_when_trail_is_unkeyed asserts the warning fires explicitly.
94-    "ignore:run_study provenance trail is UNKEYED:UserWarning",
95-    # The no-recorder gate-node advisory (#F-007) likewise fires in many graph tests that drive a
96-    # gate node without a provenance recorder; test_make_gate_node_warns_when_no_recorder asserts
97-    # it explicitly.
98-    "ignore:make_gate_node:UserWarning",
99-    # The presence-only pre-registration advisory (#G-006) fires whenever a confirm primitive is
100-    # called with authority=None — most confirm unit tests do exactly that; one explicit test
101-    # (test_confirm_primitive_warns_on_presence_only_gate) asserts it fires.
102-    "ignore:confirm primitive called with authority=None:UserWarning",
103-]
104-

"""Integration-test helpers — exercise the REAL backends, skip cleanly when absent.

These tests run the actual seams against real langgraph / mlflow / opentelemetry / ollama /
qdrant / postgres rather than mocks, so the backend code paths (the per-seam audits' coverage
gaps) are genuinely executed. Each skips when its extra or live service is unavailable, so the
dependency-free `.[dev]` lane still passes; the extras CI lane runs them for real.
"""

from __future__ import annotations

import importlib.util
import socket
import urllib.request

import pytest


def have(module: str) -> bool:
    return importlib.util.find_spec(module) is not None


def tcp_open(host: str, port: int, timeout: float = 1.0) -> bool:
    try:
        with socket.create_connection((host, port), timeout=timeout):
            return True
    except OSError:
        return False


def http_ok(url: str, timeout: float = 2.0) -> bool:
    try:
        with urllib.request.urlopen(url, timeout=timeout) as resp:
            return 200 <= resp.status < 500
    except Exception:
        return False


def ollama_up() -> bool:
    return have("langchain_ollama") and http_ok("http://localhost:11434/api/tags")


def qdrant_up() -> bool:
    return have("qdrant_client") and tcp_open("localhost", 6333)


def postgres_up() -> bool:
    return have("psycopg") and tcp_open("localhost", 5432)

=== baseline/primitives.py head ===
"""Dependency-light, deterministic numeric primitives — the baseline's raw material.

These are the small, exact, NumPy-free building blocks the methodology calls "raw material"
for a baseline: similarity/distance and descriptive statistics over a
:class:`~assay_engine.contracts.features.FeatureMatrix`. They are deterministic (no RNG, no
iteration-order dependence) and pure, so a study can assemble a simple baseline — and the
engine can unit-test these invariants — with no heavy dependency.

Studies needing performance or richer ML (embedding models, clustering, graph/topology) bring
their own builders implementing :class:`~assay_engine.baseline.toolkit.BaselineBuilder`; the
engine deliberately does not prescribe those choices (ADR-0002). Heavier engine helpers may be
added later under the optional ``baseline`` extra, but the contract here stays dependency-free.
"""

from __future__ import annotations

import math
from typing import Any, Sequence

Vector = Sequence[float]


def _check_same_length(a: Vector, b: Vector) -> None:
    if len(a) != len(b):
        raise ValueError(f"vectors must have equal length; got {len(a)} and {len(b)}")


def _check_finite(*vectors: Vector) -> None:
    """Reject non-finite components. Without this a NaN/inf could flow into cosine and be
    silently clamped to a false 1.0 (audit #B6); consistent with descriptive_stats."""

Since the CI runs `on: push: branches: [main]` and `pull_request`, the merge commit's CI is the post-merge run going red. The merges happened anyway — proving there is no enforced pre-merge gate. This is the literal mechanism by which "CI was silently red for ~3 passes."

This is my strongest, fully-falsifiable finding. The rest of the CI is genuinely clean. Let me also note one secondary concrete item: the `core` lane runs `pytest -q` with NO `--cov-fail-under`, and coverage is measured ONLY in the integration lane — meaning code reachable only in the no-extras path that the integration lane exercises differently isn't independently floored. But that's minor and by-design. The license-gate UNKNOWN-license warning (huey/skops) is a warn-not-fail, also by design.

Let me write up findings.

findings.title: No enforced required status check: red CI does not block merges (the exact mechanism behind "silently red for 3 passes")
findings.location: .github/workflows/ci.yml (all-checks job, lines ~begin of `all-checks:`) + repo branch-protection config
findings.claim: The CI design comment states "branch protection should require ONLY all-checks" and the all-checks fan-in job exists to be that single gate. But branch protection is NOT enabled/available on this repo (gh api repos/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/branches/main/protection returns HTTP 403 "Upgrade to GitHub Pro or make this repository public to enable this feature"). Therefore all-checks (and core/integration/audit) gate nothing at merge time. `gh run list --branch main` shows merge commits for PRs #161, #162, #166, #167, #168 all with CI conclusion=failure — i.e. they were merged to main and CI then ran red on the merge commit. CI runs `on: push: branches:[main]` and `pull_request`, so a failing PR run never prevented the merge.
findings.why_real: Falsifiable from two independent signals: (1) the branch-protection API explicitly reports the feature is unavailable on this plan, so no required check can be configured; (2) the run history lists 5 main-branch merge commits with conclusion=failure, proving merges of red branches actually occurred. This is the precise failure mode the pass-9 dimension was created to catch: the all-checks gate is purely decorative without an enforced required status check, so CI can be (and was) silently red across passes while merges proceeded.
findings.suggested_fix: Either make the repo public / upgrade the plan and configure branch protection on main to require the `all-checks` status check (and disallow merging PRs whose checks are not green), or — since branch protection is unavailable — change the workflow trigger strategy so the gate is enforceable: gate on PR check status before merge via the merge tooling (e.g. `gh pr merge --auto` only after checks pass), and add a self-check that fails the run if it detects it ran on a push to main with a non-success result that was not pre-gated. At minimum, document in the hardening protocol that merges must be blocked manually until `all-checks` is green, since nothing in the platform enforces it.

findings.title: Coverage floor enforced only in the all-extras integration lane; the no-extras core lane has no coverage gate
findings.location: .github/workflows/ci.yml — core job `Test (pytest)` step (`pytest -q`) vs integration job `pytest -q --cov=assay_engine --cov-fail-under=90`
findings.claim: Coverage with --cov-fail-under=90 runs ONLY in the integration lane (all extras + service containers). The core lane runs bare `pytest -q` with no coverage measurement. Coverage of the package is therefore reported under the full-backend configuration; the pure-Python fallback branches that execute in the no-extras core lane (e.g. baseline primitives' pure path, import-guard NOOP branches) are credited by the integration run's measurement of the installed-backend paths, not by an independent no-extras measurement.
findings.why_real: Verifiable from the two job step commands. It is a genuine (if minor) gap: the coverage gate cannot detect regressions in core-lane-only code paths, because the only lane that computes coverage is the one where backends are present and many fallbacks are skipped. It will not cause CI to go red; it is a measurement-blindspot, hence low severity.
findings.suggested_fix: Run coverage in the core lane too (e.g. `pytest -q --cov=assay_engine --cov-report=xml` on at least the 3.12 core leg) and combine with the integration run, or accept the gap explicitly with a comment. If kept single-lane, document that the floor reflects the all-extras configuration only.

content.text:
I completed an adversarial CI-execution review of the hc-assay engine on harden/pass-9.

What I verified (and found clean):
- No module-level unguarded backend imports in any test file. The core lane runs bare `pytest -q` collecting `tests/` including `tests/integration/`, and every backend touch is either behind a module-level `pytest.importorskip` (test_orchestration_live.py:16, test_pipeline_live.py:14, test_reasoning_drain.py:20) or deferred into guarded test bodies / `find_spec` branches. `src/assay_engine` has zero top-level backend imports.
- All three lockfiles are actually in sync with pyproject under the pinned uv 0.11.21 and `.uv-exclude-newer=2026-06-14` (I reproduced the exact audit gate commands: full, core, and build locks all diff-clean). This is the gate that broke passes 4-7.
- `--no-build-isolation --no-deps -e .` is consistent: build-system requires only hatchling, and requirements-build.lock supplies hatchling + all its runtime deps.
- The latest main run (pass-8 merge, 27701702497) is green across core 3.11-3.14, integration, audit, all-checks.
- httpx (the one importorskip-gated test backend not obvious in pyproject) is present in requirements.lock, so that test does run in the integration lane.

Genuine findings returned:
- CI-1 (high): branch protection is unavailable on this plan (the protection API returns HTTP 403), so the `all-checks` fan-in gate enforces nothing. `gh run list --branch main` shows merge commits for PRs #161/#162/#166/#167/#168 with conclusion=failure — red branches were merged and CI went red on main afterward. This is the literal mechanism behind "silently red for ~3 passes."
- CI-2 (low): the `--cov-fail-under=90` floor runs only in the all-extras integration lane; the no-extras core lane runs coverage-free, leaving core-only fallback paths unmeasured.

No invented findings — the rest of the CI is genuinely solid.
